feedback.text: Jean Monnet Modules estudiant “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers Harmonised’, No 101239437. Proyecto financiado por la Unión Europea. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH summoning notice. As a student with a major in marketing and communication, being able to participate in a public legal consultation on the future European Digital Fairness Act is a unique opportunity for me to fully understand two often distant perspectives, but which should be complementary: economic and legal. Two areas which, by pursuing different objectives, may sometimes come into ideological conflict, with possible negative consequences for the consumer. This initiative has allowed me to listen to the different views on issues that, on the business side, are often not even deceptive, from the point of view of the field that will operate today and in the future in the business world. Instead, it is crucial to become aware of this in order to build a digital future based on transparency and mutual trust between business and consumers. One of the issues that has emerged from the debate is that of misleading interfaces with dark patterns, which are based on the subtle boundary between legality and unfair competition. The most common examples are the acceptance of cookies, terms and conditions or marketing profiling, often perceived as opaque and poorly understood. The debate revealed a general confusion about the meaning and implications of each of these instruments: many consumers would not be able to define them precisely. This opens up a very important topic: are these tools perceived as misleading only because the consumer does not actually have the means to recognise them? Would it be different if the consumer had real digital education? A consumer who does not understand the digital world they interact with is a vulnerable consumer, exposed to scams and manipulations. What has emerged is that the average consumer is actually convinced that he/she is more aware and protected than it is really, which on the one hand strengthens trust in the European protection system, but also raises concerns about the growing lack of critical thinking in the use of
